title: DMA: Moving Data Without the CPU

lessons.title: What DMA Is and Why It Exists
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Imagine you are cooking dinner, and you also have to carry every single grocery bag in from the car yourself. Every trip to the car is time you are not cooking. Now imagine a delivery service that brings the bags in for you while you keep cooking, and just rings the doorbell once when everything has arrived. That delivery service is what **DMA** does for a computer.

**Build.** In a microcontroller (the small computer chip, or **MCU**, you met in earlier sub-topics), the **CPU** is the part that runs your program. Normally, when data needs to move from a peripheral (like the ADC that measures a voltage, or the UART that receives bytes) into the chip's memory (**RAM**), the CPU has to copy it one piece at a time: read a value, write it to memory, repeat. For a few bytes that is fine. But for a big block of data, or a fast stream, the CPU spends all its time just shuffling bytes and can't do anything else.

**DMA** stands for **Direct Memory Access**. It is a small helper engine built into the chip whose only job is to move data between a peripheral and memory (or between two places in memory) *on its own*, without the CPU touching each byte. You tell it three basic things: where to copy *from* (the **source**), where to copy *to* (the **destination**), and how many items to move (the **count**). Then it does the whole job by itself while the CPU does other useful work. When the move is finished, DMA pokes the CPU with an **interrupt** (a signal that says "done!"), much like the delivery service ringing the doorbell.

**Recap.** DMA is a data-moving helper. It exists to free the CPU from copying data byte by byte: the CPU sets up the transfer once, the DMA engine moves the whole block, and the CPU only gets interrupted at the end.

items.prompt: What does "DMA" stand for?
choices: Direct Memory Access
Data Management Array
Dynamic Memory Allocation
Dual Mode Addressing
items.explanation: DMA = Direct Memory Access. It is a helper engine that accesses (reads and writes) memory directly, on its own, instead of making the CPU do every copy. The other phrases sound plausible but are not what DMA means.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what does a DMA engine do?
choices: It makes the CPU run at a faster clock speed
It moves data between a peripheral and memory (or memory to memory) on its own, without the CPU copying each item
It stores extra programs when flash memory is full
It decides which interrupt the CPU should handle first
items.explanation: DMA's whole job is moving data by itself so the CPU doesn't have to. It does not change the CPU's clock, store programs, or set interrupt priorities (that is the job of other hardware).

items.prompt: Why would you use DMA instead of having the CPU copy the data itself?
choices: DMA copies data more accurately, so it makes fewer mistakes
DMA uses less RAM than the CPU does
Copying byte by byte ties up the CPU; DMA frees the CPU to do other work while data moves
DMA is the only way to read from a peripheral at all
items.explanation: The point of DMA is to offload the busywork of copying. The CPU is freed for real work while the DMA engine moves the data. The CPU can also read peripherals directly; DMA just saves it from doing the tedious copying.

items.prompt: In the grocery analogy, the CPU is the cook and DMA is the delivery service. What does the delivery service "ringing the doorbell once" represent?
choices: The CPU resetting the chip
The interrupt that DMA sends to say the transfer is finished
The peripheral running out of data
The RAM becoming full
items.explanation: The doorbell is the completion interrupt: one signal at the end telling the CPU the data has all arrived. The cook (CPU) keeps cooking the whole time and only stops to respond when the bell rings.

items.prompt: Which three basic things do you give a DMA engine to set up a transfer?
choices: Clock speed, voltage, and temperature
Source address, destination address, and count (how many items)
Username, password, and file name
Interrupt number, priority, and stack size
items.explanation: At its simplest a transfer is: copy FROM the source, TO the destination, this MANY items. Those are the core settings. The other lists are unrelated to describing a data move.

items.prompt: During a DMA transfer, how often does the CPU normally get involved?
choices: Once per item moved, the same as if it copied them itself
Continuously, watching every byte go by
Only at the edges: once to set it up, and once when it finishes
Never; the CPU is completely unaware DMA exists
items.explanation: The CPU configures the transfer, then is free during the whole move, and is interrupted only when it completes. If it had to act per item there would be no benefit, and it does have to know about DMA to set it up and handle the "done" signal.

items.prompt: A second everyday picture of DMA is a conveyor belt feeding parts to a worker. What is the lesson of that picture?
choices: The worker (CPU) doesn't have to walk over and fetch each part; the belt (DMA) brings them automatically
The conveyor belt makes the parts smaller
Only one part can ever exist at a time
The worker must turn the belt by hand
items.explanation: The conveyor belt delivers parts on its own so the worker keeps working. That mirrors DMA delivering data so the CPU keeps computing. The belt doesn't change the parts or need hand-cranking.

items.prompt: For which situation is DMA the most clearly worthwhile?
choices: Writing a single byte to one pin, once
Moving a large or fast continuous stream of data, like many ADC samples
Adding two numbers together
Deciding the order of two interrupts
items.explanation: DMA shines for big or streaming transfers, where copying byte by byte would swamp the CPU. For a single tiny write the setup overhead isn't worth it, and arithmetic or interrupt ordering are not data-moving jobs.

items.prompt: Which statement about DMA is TRUE?
choices: DMA replaces the CPU entirely
DMA can only ever copy data between two spots in memory
DMA is a separate engine that moves data while the CPU does other work
DMA requires the CPU to copy each item but in a faster way
items.explanation: DMA is a separate helper engine working alongside the CPU. It does not replace the CPU, it can move data to/from peripherals as well as memory-to-memory, and it specifically avoids making the CPU copy each item.

lessons.title: How a DMA Transfer Is Described: Source, Destination, Count, and Triggers
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A DMA engine is dumb in a good way: it only does exactly what you describe. So learning DMA mostly means learning the short list of settings that describe a transfer, and the few common shapes those transfers take.

**Build.** A DMA controller usually has several **channels**. A channel is one independent "lane" that can run its own transfer. Having several lanes means different peripherals can be served at the same time. Each channel is told:

- **Source address** — where to read from.
- **Destination address** — where to write to.
- **Count** — how many items to move.
- **Item width** — how big each item is (a byte, a half-word, or a word). This must match the data; a 12-bit ADC reading needs a half-word, not a byte.
- **Increment** — for each side, does the address step forward after every item, or stay put? A memory buffer usually *increments* (each item lands in the next slot). A single peripheral register usually does *not* increment (every item reads or writes the same fixed address).

There are three common transfer shapes. **Peripheral-to-memory** drains a peripheral into a buffer (for example, ADC samples flowing into RAM). **Memory-to-peripheral** is the reverse (sending bytes out a UART, or a waveform to a DAC). **Memory-to-memory** copies one region of RAM to another, like a hardware version of `memcpy`.

Most useful DMA is **peripheral-triggered**: instead of running flat out, the engine waits for the peripheral to say "I have something." The ADC says "a sample is ready," or the UART says "a byte arrived," or a timer ticks. Each such event moves exactly one item. That ties the data rate to real hardware events. Memory-to-memory has no such event, so it just runs as fast as the bus allows.

**Recap.** A transfer is fully described by source, destination, count, item width, and increment settings, plus what triggers it. Get the width and increment right or the data quietly comes out garbled.

items.prompt: What is a DMA "channel"?
choices: A wire that carries the chip's main clock
One independent transfer lane that can run its own DMA transfer
A region of flash memory reserved for DMA
The interrupt priority assigned to the CPU
items.explanation: A channel is an independent transfer engine/lane with its own settings. Having several lets different peripherals be served at once. It is not a clock, a flash region, or an interrupt priority.

items.prompt: Which set of fields describes a basic DMA transfer?
choices: Source address, destination address, count, item width, and increment settings
Voltage, current, resistance, and capacitance
Username, file size, and timestamp
Baud rate, parity, and stop bits only
items.explanation: Those five things fully describe where data comes from, where it goes, how much, how wide each item is, and whether each side's address steps forward. The other options describe electrical quantities, files, or only UART framing.

items.prompt: When DMA captures ADC samples into a RAM buffer, which addresses increment?
choices: Both the ADC register and the buffer increment
Neither increments
The memory buffer increments; the ADC data register stays fixed
The ADC register increments; the buffer stays fixed
items.explanation: Each new sample should land in the next buffer slot, so the buffer (destination) increments. The ADC's data register is one fixed address you re-read each time, so its increment is off.

items.prompt: You set the item width to a single byte (8 bits) but the ADC produces 12-bit results. What is the likely outcome?
choices: The chip raises an error and stops
The data is silently garbled, with no error reported
DMA automatically switches to the correct width
The transfer count doubles to make up for it
items.explanation: A width that doesn't match the data does NOT cause a fault; it just misreads the register and corrupts the buffer quietly. A 12-bit result needs a half-word. This is why matching width to the data matters so much.

items.prompt: Sending bytes out of a UART using DMA is which transfer shape?
choices: Memory-to-peripheral
Peripheral-to-memory
Memory-to-memory
Peripheral-to-peripheral
items.explanation: You are reading from a memory buffer and writing to the UART's transmit register, so data flows memory-to-peripheral. Peripheral-to-memory would be the reverse (receiving), and memory-to-memory involves no peripheral.

items.prompt: What does "peripheral-triggered DMA" mean?
choices: DMA runs as fast as possible, ignoring the peripheral
The CPU must trigger each item by hand
The peripheral signals an event (sample ready, byte received, timer tick) and the engine moves one item per event
DMA only starts after the program ends
items.explanation: Peripheral-triggered means a hardware event paces the transfer: one event moves one item. This couples the data rate to real hardware timing rather than a software loop. Running flat out describes memory-to-memory instead.

items.prompt: Which transfer typically runs "as fast as the bus allows" rather than waiting for an event?
choices: ADC samples flowing into memory
Bytes streaming out of a UART
A memory-to-memory copy
A timer-paced DAC playback
items.explanation: Memory-to-memory has no external event to wait for, so it bursts along limited only by bus availability. The other three are each paced by a peripheral or timer event.

items.prompt: Why might a DMA controller have several channels instead of just one?
choices: To store more data inside the DMA engine
So multiple peripherals can have transfers running at the same time
Because each channel uses a different voltage
To make the CPU run faster
items.explanation: Each channel is an independent lane, so the ADC, the UART, and a DAC can all have transfers in progress concurrently. Channels don't store data, change voltage, or speed up the CPU.

items.prompt: When DMA writes to a single fixed peripheral register (like a DAC output), the destination increment should be:
choices: On, so the address walks past the register
Off, so every item writes the same fixed register address
Set to match the count
Irrelevant for that kind of transfer
items.explanation: The destination is one register, so its address must stay put (increment off) while the memory source increments through the data. Turning it on would write into neighboring registers and cause bugs.

items.prompt: A timer triggers the ADC, which triggers DMA. What then sets the exact moment each sample is taken?
choices: How busy the CPU happens to be
The timer's hardware trigger rate, independent of CPU load
The size of the RAM buffer
The UART baud rate
items.explanation: With hardware triggering, the timer defines the precise, even sample instant regardless of what the CPU is doing. This is exactly the evenly-sampled buffer the ADC and timer sub-topics described, now collected without CPU babysitting.

lessons.title: Continuous Streaming: Circular Buffers, Ping-Pong, and the Stale-Data Trap
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A single DMA transfer is great, but real sensors stream forever. You want to keep collecting data while also processing what you already collected, without ever stopping or losing a sample. Two ideas make that possible, and one sneaky hazard can ruin it.

**Build.** First, **circular mode**. Normally DMA stops when the count runs out. In circular mode, the moment it reaches the end of the buffer it automatically jumps back to the start and keeps going. The stream never stops, and the CPU never has to re-arm it.

Second, **ping-pong** (also called **double buffering**). Think of two plates: while you eat from plate A, the kitchen fills plate B; when A is empty you switch and eat from B while A refills. With DMA, you treat the buffer as two halves. DMA fills half A while the CPU processes half B, then they swap. The CPU always works on the half the DMA is *not* currently writing, so there are never torn or half-written reads.

To know when to swap, DMA gives you two interrupts: the **half-transfer (HT)** interrupt fires when the first half is full, and the **transfer-complete (TC)** interrupt fires when the second half is full (and, in circular mode, wraps around). These are the same kind of "something happened" interrupts you met in the interrupts sub-topic.

**The hazard.** DMA writes RAM *behind the CPU's back*. That causes a coherency problem on two levels. The compiler may not realize the memory changed, so a DMA buffer and its "done" flag must be marked **`volatile`** (a hint that says "always re-read this from memory, it can change on its own"). On fancier chips with a data **cache** (like the Cortex-M7), there is a second layer: the CPU might read an old cached copy instead of what DMA just wrote, so you must do **cache maintenance** (invalidate the buffer before reading) or place the buffer in a non-cacheable region. Because cache operations work on whole chunks (lines), DMA buffers should be properly **aligned and sized**. Finally, DMA and the CPU share the memory **bus**, so a very heavy DMA stream can slow the CPU down by competing for bandwidth.

**Recap.** Circular mode keeps the stream running; ping-pong lets you process one half while the other fills; HT and TC interrupts tell you when to swap. Always remember DMA changes memory on its own, so use `volatile`, mind cache coherency on cached chips, and keep buffers aligned.

items.prompt: What does DMA "circular mode" do?
choices: It moves data in a circular pattern around the chip
When the buffer fills, it automatically wraps back to the start and keeps going
It stops the transfer and waits for the CPU to restart it
It rotates the bits inside each item
items.explanation: Circular mode auto-wraps the pointer to the buffer start at the end of the count, so the stream runs continuously with no CPU re-arming. It does not move data in circles or rotate bits.

items.prompt: What is ping-pong (double) buffering FOR?
choices: To let the CPU process one half of the data while DMA fills the other half, with no stopping or torn reads
To make the buffer twice as fast
To send the same data twice for safety
To bounce data between two different chips
items.explanation: Ping-pong splits the buffer in two so processing and collecting happen at the same time on different halves, like eating from one plate while the other refills. It is not about speed, duplication, or two chips.

items.prompt: In ping-pong buffering, which half does the CPU process?
choices: Whichever half DMA is currently writing
The half DMA is NOT currently writing
Both halves at once
Neither; the CPU waits until DMA fully stops
items.explanation: The CPU always works on the half DMA is not touching, so it never reads half-written (torn) data. Processing the half being written would risk reading data that is still arriving.

items.prompt: The half-transfer (HT) interrupt tells you that:
choices: A bus error happened
The first half of the buffer is now full and ready to process
Exactly one item was moved
DMA has stopped permanently
items.explanation: HT fires at the midpoint: the first half is complete and stable, so the CPU can process it while DMA continues into the second half. It is not an error, a per-item signal, or a stop.

items.prompt: The transfer-complete (TC) interrupt tells you that:
choices: The chip needs to reset
The second half is full (and in circular mode, DMA wraps back to the first half)
The first half is full
The CPU is overloaded
items.explanation: TC fires at the end of the buffer: the second half is ready to process, and in circular mode the engine immediately wraps to refill the first half. HT, not TC, signals the first half.

items.prompt: Why must a DMA buffer (and its "done" flag) usually be marked `volatile` in C?
choices: `volatile` makes the transfer faster
Because DMA can change the memory on its own, and `volatile` forces the program to always re-read it instead of using a stale cached-in-register copy
`volatile` encrypts the buffer
It is required for all variables in embedded code
items.explanation: DMA writes memory independently of the CPU. Without `volatile`, the compiler might keep an old value in a CPU register and never notice the change. `volatile` says "always re-read from memory." It does not speed up or encrypt anything.

items.prompt: On a chip with a data cache (like a Cortex-M7), DMA fills a buffer in RAM but the CPU keeps reading the old values. The usual cause is:
choices: The DMA priority was too low
The CPU read a stale copy from its cache; the buffer's cache was not invalidated before reading
Circular mode was off
The buffer was declared too large
items.explanation: DMA writes RAM directly, but the CPU's cache may still hold the old copy. You must invalidate (discard) that cached region before reading, or mark it non-cacheable, so the CPU fetches the fresh data DMA wrote.

items.prompt: Does marking a buffer `volatile` solve the data-cache coherency problem on a Cortex-M7?
choices: Yes, `volatile` handles both the compiler and the hardware cache
No; `volatile` only stops the compiler caching in registers. The hardware cache still needs maintenance (invalidate/clean) or a non-cacheable region
Yes, but only if circular mode is on
No, because `volatile` doesn't exist on that chip
items.explanation: These are two separate layers. `volatile` is a compiler hint; the hardware data cache is a different thing that needs explicit invalidate/clean operations or a non-cacheable memory region. Getting one right does not fix the other.

items.prompt: Why should DMA buffers on a cached chip be properly aligned and sized to whole cache lines?
choices: To make the buffer look neater in memory
Because cache operations act on whole lines; if a buffer shares a line with another variable, invalidating can wipe out that neighbor's fresh value
Because DMA cannot read odd addresses
It is only a style preference with no real effect
items.explanation: Cache maintenance works per line (a fixed chunk of bytes). If an unrelated variable shares the buffer's line, invalidating the buffer can discard that neighbor's data too. Aligning and padding to whole lines keeps the buffer alone in its lines.

items.prompt: An alternative to doing cache maintenance every time is to:
choices: Turn off all interrupts during the transfer
Place the buffer in a memory region configured as non-cacheable
Use only byte-width transfers
Set the DMA channel to the highest priority
items.explanation: If the buffer's region is non-cacheable, the CPU always reads straight from RAM, so no invalidate/clean is needed. The trade-off is slower CPU access to that region. The other options don't address cache staleness.

items.prompt: A heavy DMA stream is running and a time-critical CPU task suddenly takes longer than usual. What is the likely mechanism?
choices: DMA lowers the CPU's clock speed
DMA and the CPU share the memory bus, so heavy DMA traffic competes for bandwidth and slows the CPU's memory accesses
DMA turns off the CPU's cache
DMA deletes the CPU's program
items.explanation: DMA and the CPU both use the same memory bus. When DMA is moving a lot of data, the CPU's loads and stores may have to wait, increasing worst-case timing. DMA does not change the clock, disable the cache, or delete code.
